data.title: Middle East conflict to add over 700 business insolvency cases in U.S. - Allianz
data.description:
The Middle East conflict is expected to add over 700 business insolvency cases in the U.S. in 2026, and over 200 cases in 2027, according to a report by Allianz Research.

The crisis is expected to make 2026 the fifth consecutive year of rising global business insolvencies.

U.S. is expected to extend its rise in insolvencies to +9% in 2026 from +7% in 2025.

The extended rise in business insolvencies is estimated to put 2.2M jobs directly at risk globally in 2026, followed by a marginal decrease in 2027 of 34K.

Related ETFs: (USMC), (USA), (USPX), (USMV), (USMF), (USVM), (USHY), (USFR), (USIG), (UST).

data.title: P&G beats Q3 and rises 3%, but the valuation grade says it's still expensive at a D rating
data.description:
Procter & Gamble's (PG) solid fiscal Q3 beat sent shares up 3.3% on Friday, with organic sales of 3% topping estimates and non-GAAP EPS of $1.59 clearing the $1.56 consensus.

Despite the strong quarter, the household products giant carries a D valuation grade, placing it firmly in Sell territory relative to its fundamentals, and ranking fifth out of eight stocks on this list.

At a D grade, the stock is considered expensive relative to its fundamentals even after Friday's beat, while sector peers Colgate-Palmolive (CL) fares even worse with a D-, and Church & Dwight (CHD) and WD-40 Company (WDFC) both sit at the bottom with F grades, the most overvalued readings on the entire list. The read-through peers that moved higher alongside P&G on Friday, CHD, CLX, and CL, occupy very different positions on the valuation spectrum.

At the other end of the scale, Clorox (CLX) and Reynolds Consumer Products (REYN) stand out as the best-valued names in the sector, both earning B grades — the highest on the list and squarely in Buy territory. Central Garden & Pet (CENT) follows with a B-, rounding out the three names where valuation grades suggest the stock is reasonably priced relative to fundamentals. Kimberly-Clark (KMB) sits in the middle with a C+, representing a more neutral valuation picture.

The valuation grade reflects how expensive a stock is relative to its fundamentals. A grade of B- or above is considered a Buy signal, while D+ or below indicates a Sell, and F represents the most overvalued end of the spectrum.

Here is the list:

  1. The Clorox Company (CLX), Valuation grade: B

  2. Reynolds Consumer Products Inc. (REYN), Valuation grade: B

  3. Central Garden & Pet Company (CENT), Valuation grade: B-

  4. Kimberly-Clark Corporation (KMB), Valuation grade: C+

  5. The Procter & Gamble Company (PG), Valuation grade: D

  6. Colgate-Palmolive Company (CL), Valuation grade: D-

  7. Church & Dwight Co., Inc. (CHD), Valuation grade: F

  8. WD-40 Company (WDFC), Valuation grade: F

Consumer Staples ETFs: (XLP), (VDC), (IYK), (FSTA), (KXI), and (RSPS)

data.title: Norfolk Southern maintains $8.2B-$8.4B 2026 cost envelope while targeting $150M-plus efficiencies amid fuel volatility
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Norfolk Southern (NSC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We successfully navigated another challenging winter with weather events that affected most of our territory" and "exited the quarter with solid momentum" while "staying focused on what matters most, operating the railroad safely" (President, CEO & Director Mark George).
  • "Total adjusted expenses were up just 1% year-over-year despite inflationary pressures, storm costs and sharply higher fuel prices" (CEO George).
  • "We are advancing and layering progressive PSR 2.0 structural changes" and "in the first quarter, we achieved a fuel efficiency record" (COO & Executive VP John Orr).
  • "Overall, volume finished down 1%" and "revenue ended the quarter flat year-over-year and RPU was up 2%" (Executive VP & Chief Commercial Officer Ed Elkins).
  • "We incurred $52 million in merger-related expenses during the quarter, while total costs related to the Eastern Ohio incident were $10 million" and "adjusting for these items, the operating ratio for the quarter was 68.7% and EPS was $2.65 per share" (Executive VP & CFO Jason Zampi).

Outlook

  • "Last quarter, we provided an adjusted operating cost envelope of $8.2 billion to $8.4 billion for 2026" and "we are maintaining our current cost guidance while acknowledging the near-term volatility and uncertainty on one of our key cost inputs" (CEO George).
  • "Fuel is obviously going to be a wildcard for the remainder of the year" and "we anticipate it to be a headwind in the second quarter" while "we expect to achieve typical margin seasonality from 1Q to 2Q" (CFO Zampi).
  • "We remain on track to refile the application by the end of the month" and "this revised application will be even stronger in articulating the benefits of creating the nation's first single-line transcontinental railroad" (CEO George).

Financial Results

  • "Adjusting for these items, the operating ratio for the quarter was 68.7% and EPS was $2.65 per share" (CFO Zampi).
  • "Fuel price alone was $31 million higher than last year and over $40 million higher than our expectations" (CFO Zampi).
  • "We have continued to deliver on our productivity initiatives with fuel efficiency and labor productivity delivering over $30 million in savings" (CFO Zampi).
  • "Overall, volume finished down 1%" and "revenue ended the quarter flat year-over-year" (CCO Elkins).

Q&A

  • Christian Wetherbee, Wells Fargo: asked about "normal OR seasonality" and competitive impacts tied to the merger; CFO Zampi said sequential OR improvement was expected at "about 200 basis points" and CCO Elkins said competitive activity was "primarily an intermodal story."
  • Scott Group, Wolfe Research: pressed on merchandise pricing and merger approval confidence; CCO Elkins said "RPU, of course, is not price" and mix was driven by "growth in some of the lower-rated chemicals commodities," while CEO George said "I'm feeling a lot better" and the refile would have "a much stronger set of data."
  • Brian Ossenbeck, JPMorgan: asked for fuel and storm costs and what improved market outlook markers meant; CFO Zampi cited fuel up "$31 million" vs. last year and storm costs of "about $13 million to $15 million," while CCO Elkins said NS had "over 400 or so projects" in the pipeline and "12 projects come online in Q1... worth about 70,000 loads when they're at full ramp."
  • Jason Seidl, TD Cowen: asked about peak season intermodal discussions and whether the Jaguar partnership is replicable; CCO Elkins said he was "bullish on domestic non-premium intermodal" and "we're going to look to replicate this sort of deal elsewhere."
  • Jonathan Chappell, Evercore ISI: questioned fuel consumption and headcount step-down; COO Orr said sequential fuel improvement had "some accounting adjustments" but was driven by "treating fuel as a major cost lever," while CEO George highlighted the complexity of managing staffing across "some 90 different crew bases."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to skeptical on pricing durability, intermodal share/service levels, and cost trajectory, reflected in challenges such as "I would have thought or hoped we'd see some better merchandise pricing" (Scott Group, Wolfe Research) and questions on whether service needs to be "above 90%" to regain share (Jordan Alliger, Goldman Sachs).
  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks but more defensive and explanatory in Q&A, emphasizing mix and execution, including "RPU, of course, is not price" (CCO Elkins) and "fuel is obviously going to be a wildcard" (CFO Zampi), while reiterating confidence in sequential OR improvement of "about 200 basis points" (CFO Zampi).
  • Versus the prior quarter, the call’s tension shifted more toward fuel-driven uncertainty and near-term competitive intermodal pressure, while merger messaging leaned more confident, with CEO George saying "I feel better... than I did even 5 months ago" compared with Q4’s emphasis on STB completeness and process.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language stayed centered on the same 2026 cost envelope, but Q1 added sharper fuel emphasis: Q4 said costs would be "$8.2 billion to $8.4 billion" with flexibility, while Q1 said it was "unclear on how long fuel prices will remain inflated" and the company would "update you accordingly" (CEO George).
  • Financial bridge factors rotated: Q4 highlighted an "$85 million" land-sale benefit to operating expenses and normalized land sales ahead; Q1 highlighted fuel as "$31 million" higher vs. last year and "over $40 million higher" than expectations (CFO Zampi).
  • Commercial focus moved from Q4’s restrained intermodal view to Q1’s more constructive domestic intermodal stance amid higher diesel and tightening truck capacity, with CCO Elkins saying "we have an optimistic view of intermodal" while "tempering... due to increased competitor activity."

Risks and Concerns

  • "Conflict in Iran... driven energy prices sharply upward" and management said fuel surcharge revenue would be "the most immediate impact as an offset to fuel expense" while fuel remains a "wildcard" (CCO Elkins; CFO Zampi).
  • Competitive responses tied to the merger remained a live issue, with CFO Zampi referencing "revenue losses from the competitive merger responses" and CCO Elkins saying it was "primarily an intermodal story."
  • Weather disruption persisted as an operational and cost risk, with storm costs of "about $13 million to $15 million" and volume disruption in February described as severe winter weather impacting car loadings "for several weeks" (CFO Zampi; CCO Elkins).

Final Takeaway

Norfolk Southern’s management framed Q1 as a quarter of weather disruption and flat revenue but continued cost control, with adjusted expenses up 1% year-over-year and adjusted results of a 68.7% operating ratio and $2.65 EPS. Leadership maintained the $8.2B-$8.4B 2026 adjusted operating cost envelope while calling fuel a key near-term uncertainty, citing March fuel expense running more than $40M above expectations. Commercial commentary highlighted intermodal competitive pressure tied to the pending UP merger process alongside momentum in chemicals and automotive share, and management reiterated plans to refile the merger application by month-end with a stronger data package.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Western Union reaffirms 2026 EPS of $1.75-$1.85 while targeting $150M Travel Money revenue and launching USDPT next month
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Western Union (WU) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "In the first quarter, we reported revenue of $1 billion. On an adjusted basis, this was a decline of 1% year-over-year." (President, CEO & Director Devin McGranahan)
  • "Consumer money transfer transactions were slightly positive in the quarter for the first time since Q1 of 2025" and management said it "should set us up to return to a more meaningful transaction growth beginning in the second quarter of this year." (President, CEO & Director McGranahan)
  • "Adjusted earnings per share came in at $0.25... This is below our expectations" and management tied the miss to "incremental investments associated with our strategic agent signings, product expansion, and the timing of certain expenses." (President, CEO & Director McGranahan)
  • "In response to the slow start to the year, we have decided to accelerate our operational efficiency program" and said the company expects to complete "our $150 million operating efficiency program by year-end 2028, with large contributions coming in both 2026 and 2027." (President, CEO & Director McGranahan)
  • "Adjusted operating margin was 13%" and the CFO said Q1 pressure reflected "a lack of vendor incentive payments" and "higher costs associated with our new agent signings, a foreign currency loss and the seasonal dynamics associated with our travel money business." (Executive VP & CFO Matthew Cagwin)
  • "Last month, we closed on the acquisition of Lana in Mexico" and said it "gives us the license to launch a digital wallet in the country, which we plan to do later this year on our Beyond Digital platform." (President, CEO & Director McGranahan)
  • "Earlier this month, we also completed the acquisition of Dash, Singtel's digital wallet business in Singapore" and said the company expects to close Intermex in Q2, noting, "we are now down to just one jurisdiction." (President, CEO & Director McGranahan)
  • "USDPT is now in its final stages of readiness and is expected to go live next month" and "we plan to launch our first partner on the DAN network next week" with a "Stable Card later this year." (President, CEO & Director McGranahan)

Outlook

  • "We are reaffirming our guidance, which includes our adjusted revenue outlook for 2026 at 6% to 9% revenue growth, inclusive of the Intermex acquisition" and "our adjusted EPS for the full year... between $1.75 to $1.85." (Executive VP & CFO Cagwin)
  • "We expect Q2 EPS to be similar to last year and then to accelerate as we move into the back half of the year" driven by "new agent wins" and "a seasonally stronger period for Travel Money" plus "the Intermex synergies and lower vendor and labor costs." (Executive VP & CFO Cagwin)
  • Compared with Q4, guidance ranges were unchanged: "Our adjusted revenue outlook for 2026 is for 6% to 9% revenue growth" and "adjusted EPS... between $1.75 to $1.85." (Executive VP & CFO Cagwin, Q4 2025)

Financial Results

  • "In the first quarter, GAAP revenue was $983 million" and "adjusted EPS was $0.25 in the current quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Cagwin)
  • "We generated $109 million in operating cash flow" and reported "cash and cash equivalents of $900 million and debt of $2.6 billion" with leverage of "2.8x and 1.8x on a gross and net basis." (Executive VP & CFO Cagwin)
  • "Consumer Services... contributed 14% of total revenue in the quarter" and "first quarter adjusted revenue was up 33%, driven by the expansion of our Travel Money business and growth in our Consumer Bill Pay business." (Executive VP & CFO Cagwin)
  • "CMT adjusted revenue was down 6%" while "CMT transactions were slightly positive" as "Branded Digital" grew "transactions 21%" and "adjusted revenue by 6%." (Executive VP & CFO Cagwin)
  • "In the quarter, we returned over $120 million to our owners via dividends and stock repurchases." (Executive VP & CFO Cagwin)

Q&A

  • William Nance, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division: asked what drove Q1 EPS below expectations and to size FX; Executive VP & CFO Cagwin said "about 50% of the decline year-over-year is driven by things that we anticipated" and the unanticipated items were "the FX loss" that was "multiple pennies of EPS" plus "our dual track got dislocated."
  • William Nance, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division: asked about the Middle East conflict; President, CEO & Director McGranahan said the impact has been "mixed," with "a noted decline" in Europe-to-Middle East travel money but "a moderate acceleration of outbound remittances from the Middle East."
  • Tien-Tsin Huang, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division: pressed on execution risk while integrating acquisitions and launching digital assets; President, CEO & Director McGranahan responded, "of course, there is always execution risk" and outlined levers including "sunsetting legacy platforms" and that "AI is starting to take effect."
  • Tien-Tsin Huang, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division: asked whether Lana/Dash are beachheads; President, CEO & Director McGranahan said the Beyond platform "enables us to accelerate this through acquisitions" and the CFO added, "By buying in Mexico and in Singapore that brought licenses."
  • Vasundhara Govil, Keefe, Bruyette, & Woods, Inc., Research Division: asked stablecoin go-to-market milestones; President, CEO & Director McGranahan said USDPT starts as "an alternative to the interbank SWIFT settlement network," DAN goes live "next week," and the Stable Card launches in "the next 90 to 180 days" in initial countries.
  • Bryan Keane, Citigroup Inc., Research Division: questioned digital revenue vs. transaction growth; President, CEO & Director McGranahan said revenue was pressured by "shift to payout to account" and "very aggressive new customer offers" that the company is "moving back from a bit."
  • Darrin Peller, Wolfe Research, LLC: asked why expand retail while pushing digital; President, CEO & Director McGranahan said strategy is signing large partners ("Kroger going exclusive, the Deutsche Post, the Canada Post") plus owned concept stores to "introduce people to the digital products" and cross-sell.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to skeptical, focusing on the EPS/margin miss and execution load, including "what was it that actually drove things that were below expectations" and whether "there is execution risk there." (William Nance, Goldman Sachs; Tien-Tsin Huang, JPMorgan)
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks but more defensive in Q&A; responses included "of course, there is always execution risk" and explanations such as "our dual track got dislocated" and FX was "a little bit of a surprise." (President, CEO & Director McGranahan; Executive VP & CFO Cagwin)
  • Versus Q4, management language shifted from "delivered at the top end of our earnings guidance" (Executive VP & CFO Cagwin, Q4 2025) to "below our expectations" (President, CEO & Director McGranahan) while reiterating that "we are reaffirming our guidance." (Executive VP & CFO Cagwin)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 highlighted stabilization more explicitly: "emerging stabilization we are seeing in the U.S. remittance market" (President, CEO & Director McGranahan) versus Q4’s "it may be too early to say that we have reached bottom." (President, CEO & Director McGranahan, Q4 2025)
  • Branded Digital transaction growth accelerated to "21%" in Q1 from "13%" in Q4, while adjusted revenue growth stayed at "6%" in both quarters. (President, CEO & Director McGranahan; President, CEO & Director McGranahan, Q4 2025)
  • M&A posture moved from expectation to completion: Q4 said "look forward to welcoming Intermex... hopefully in the second quarter" (President, CEO & Director McGranahan, Q4 2025) while Q1 said Lana and Dash were closed and Intermex is "down to just one jurisdiction." (President, CEO & Director McGranahan)

Risks and Concerns

  • "U.S. immigration policy uncertainty remains a meaningful headwind" and guidance assumes "no macroeconomic changes and no significant impact from the conflict in the Middle East." (Executive VP & CFO Cagwin)
  • Management cautioned that in the Middle East conflict, remittance patterns can "revert themselves if the conflict remains extended" as volumes "begin to shrink." (President, CEO & Director McGranahan)
  • The CFO flagged leverage pressure post-Intermex: "we expect our debt-to-EBITDA ratios to be elevated above historical levels for the 12 to 18 months post closing." (Executive VP & CFO Cagwin)

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as a lower-than-expected EPS quarter, but emphasized improving U.S. remittance trends, reaffirmed full-year guidance, and positioned the year’s setup around Intermex closing and cost synergies, large retail agent rollouts, Consumer Services growth led by Travel Money approaching $150 million in 2026 revenue, and near-term digital asset commercialization milestones including USDPT going live next month, the first DAN partner next week, and a Stable Card rollout later this year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Walmart dips after a six-session rally
data.description:
Shares of Walmart (WMT) fell 1.50% to $130.05 in the afternoon trade on Friday, ending a six-session winning streak.

The stock has gained around 5.8% between April 16 and April 23, compared to a 0.95% rise in the S&P 500 during the same period.

According to Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating system, WMT is rated a Hold, with a score of 3.38 out of 5, receiving an A+ for profitability, but an F in terms of valuation.

A recent Seeking Alpha analysis rated WMT a Sell, citing an unjustified surge in valuation despite slowing growth trends, and the analyst pointed out that “the stock’s valuation has gone up, in my view, in an unreasonable way.”

While on Wall Street, 38 out of 42 analysts rate the stock a buy or higher, three recommend a hold, while one of them suggests a sell or lower.

Despite the modest decline, shares remain 17.8% up year-to-date and have gained around 5.8% in the past month.

data.title: OceanFirst outlines NIM past 3% in 2026 back half while targeting mid- to high single-digit loan and deposit growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: OceanFirst Financial Corp. (OCFC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We reported solid first quarter results, which included earnings per share of $0.36 on a fully diluted GAAP basis and $0.43 on a core basis." (Chairman & CEO Christopher Maher)

  • "We delivered our fifth consecutive quarter of net interest income growth" and said net interest margin expanded "to 2.93%, supported by lower cost of funds and earning asset growth." (Chairman & CEO Maher)

  • On expense actions and technology, Maher said core operating expenses declined on outsourcing the residential platform and that the bank has "worked diligently to restructure our core IT infrastructure" and "started to see the efficiency benefits in legacy bank processes" from AI investments via vendors. (Chairman & CEO Maher)

  • On strategic priorities around New York and talent, Joseph Lebel said C&I and commercial real estate closed-loan volume was "up 81% year-over-year" and added, "We added another 3 C&I bankers in Q1 2026 with plans for more in the coming quarters." (President, COO & Director Joseph Lebel)

  • On deposits and Premier Bank, Lebel said, "Total deposits grew by $192 million or 2% in the quarter" and that Premier has brought in "over 1,500 new accounts across 400 relationships" since May 2025, with "approximately 20% representing noninterest-bearing accounts." (President Lebel)

  • On the Flushing Financial merger status, Maher said, "Approval from the Federal Reserve remains the final outstanding regulatory requirement," and the company is working toward "an expected closing in the second quarter of 2026" with "a full systems integration and rebranding in the third quarter of 2026." (Chairman & CEO Maher)

Outlook

  • Patrick Barrett said, "There are no changes to our full year guidance as stated in the previous quarter, although we have removed the modest impact of further Fed rate cuts from our outlook." (Senior EVP & CFO Patrick Barrett)

  • Barrett reiterated stand-alone guidance of "mid- to high single-digit loan and deposit growth," "NIM growing past 3% in the back half of the year," "other income ranging from $7 million to $9 million per quarter" and "expenses stable at $70 million to $71 million per quarter," adding: "Note that these are stand-alone expectations and do not reflect the impact of the Flushing acquisition." (CFO Barrett)

  • For near-term expenses, Barrett said, "we expect our second quarter core operating expense run rate to remain in the range of $70 million to $71 million." (CFO Barrett)

Financial Results

  • Maher reported quarterly performance including "earnings per share of $0.36" (GAAP) and "$0.43" (core), plus loan growth: "Total loans for the quarter increased by $92 million" driven by "$429 million in originations." (Chairman & CEO Maher)

  • Maher reported GAAP operating expenses of "$73 million, which includes $4 million of merger-related expenses," while "on a core basis, operating expenses of $69 million declined by $2.1 million or 3% from the linked quarter." (Chairman & CEO Maher)

  • On credit metrics, Barrett said "nonperforming loans to total loans and nonperforming assets to total assets, both at 0.31%," while "net charge-offs were de minimis" at "3 basis points of average total loans on an annualized basis." (CFO Barrett)

  • Lebel said noninterest income "decreased by $2.7 million to $7 million" primarily due to a lower gain on sale of loans of "$779,000" after the residential outsourcing, and cited "reductions in commercial loan swap income" tied to lower swap origination volume. (President Lebel)

Q&A

  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: asked about deposit growth sustainability and mix shift; President Lebel said, "I think you see some seasonality" and added, "we're still pretty bullish" on Premier momentum into Q2 and Q3. (President Lebel)

  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: asked why net interest income guidance holds despite removing expected cuts; CFO Barrett said, "competition is pretty intense" and noted yield pressure as "any benefit from maturities and rollovers is being competed away for new originations." (CFO Barrett)

  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: asked on Flushing portfolio sale; Maher said, "There's nothing that's changed our outlook" and "the merger model is holding up," while deferring specifics until "legal day 1." (Chairman & CEO Maher)

  • Timothy Switzer, KBW: asked about hiring goals and expense impact; Maher said, "we're really bullish on the opportunity" in New York and later added, "don't look for expenses to move up" even with more hires; CFO Barrett added, "don't be surprised if you see comp expenses go up and data processing expenses go down and the net would be a push." (Chairman & CEO Maher; CFO Barrett)

  • Timothy Switzer, KBW: asked about credit migration; Maher said it was "a single customer" and "not something that had a pattern." (Chairman & CEO Maher)

  • Timothy Switzer, KBW: asked about merger closing timing; Maher said the company will close "pretty promptly" after Fed approval but noted a typical "15 days" wait after the final approval. (Chairman & CEO Maher)

  • David Bishop, Hovde: asked about repricing Flushing deposits; Maher said, "the model was not especially dependent upon that" and described an "all-encompassing review" of assets, hedges, liabilities and securities to set the combined balance sheet. (Chairman & CEO Maher)

  • David Bishop, Hovde: asked about growth beyond New York; Lebel said OceanFirst has "almost a dozen folks" in the D.C.-Baltimore metro and is "looking for more," while Maher highlighted relationship banking opportunities with smaller government-adjacent technology firms. (President Lebel; Chairman & CEO Maher)

  • Christopher Marinac, Brean Capital: asked how non-New York C&I markets complement the combined footprint; Lebel said three Q1 C&I hires were in Boston, the Boston team is "now 8 folks or so," Philly is "north of $2 billion today," and the company is "really bullish" on those markets. (President Lebel)

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly positive overall, emphasizing execution questions on deposits, competition, credit migration, and merger timing (e.g., "how sustainable" deposit mix shift is; and whether "competition is increasing"). (Analysts)

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A on merger specifics, leaning on process timing and deferrals (e.g., "we'll talk to you then" on balance sheet restructure and portfolio actions). (Management)

  • Versus the prior quarter, the tone shifted toward regulatory-completion details and integration readiness; Q4 featured more emphasis on launching the merger narrative, while Q1 added specific milestones (NYDFS and OCC approvals) and operational day-one planning. (Current vs. previous quarter)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 emphasized margin improvement (Maher cited 2.93%) and deposit growth of $192 million, while Q4 highlighted faster loan growth ($474 million) and margin at 2.87%; both calls described ongoing net interest income growth. (Current vs. previous quarter)

  • Guidance language was maintained quarter-over-quarter as Barrett said "no changes" to full-year guidance, but Q1 added that the company "removed the modest impact of further Fed rate cuts" from the outlook. (CFO Barrett)

  • Analysts’ focus shifted from Q4 questions around credit risk transfer and Flushing deposit composition to Q1 questions centered on deposit mix sustainability, competition affecting spreads, and timing/structure decisions at merger close. (Analysts)

Risks and Concerns

  • Barrett cited "competition is pretty intense" in lending and described loan yields as pressured by pricing competition and the yield curve. (CFO Barrett)

  • Credit risk monitoring increased due to "one large commercial relationship" driving criticized/classified loan increases, with management emphasizing it "remains current and well collateralized" and that charge-offs were minimal. (CFO Barrett)

  • Merger close timing risk remained tied to the final Fed approval; Maher said the Federal Reserve is the "final outstanding" item and closing follows "their process." (Chairman & CEO Maher)

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as a stand-alone quarter of continued net interest income growth, margin expansion to 2.93%, and controlled core expenses supported by residential outsourcing and IT/AI-related efficiency efforts. Leadership reiterated full-year stand-alone targets—including mid- to high single-digit loan and deposit growth and NIM past 3% in the back half of 2026—while framing the Flushing transaction as awaiting final Federal Reserve approval ahead of a targeted Q2 2026 close and Q3 2026 systems integration and rebranding.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: S&P 500, Nasdaq rise, Dow lags after Israel-Lebanon ceasefire extension
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were mixed on Friday as investors digested the latest developments in the Middle East, including a three-week extension to the truce between Israel and Lebanon.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +0.6%, while the heavy-tech Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +1.4%. Meanwhile, the blue-chip Dow was -0.3%.

Intel (INTC) shares jumped 20.6% after its Q1 results delivered a beat, and now its second-quarter revenue outlook is expected to surpass what analysts anticipated. Other semiconductors also rose, including Nvidia (NVDA), TSMC (TSM), AMD (AMD), Arm Holdings (ARM), and Qualcomm (QCOM).

Treasury yields were lower. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 1 basis point lower at 4.32%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) dropped 6 basis points to 3.79%.

U.S. President Donald Trump announced that the ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon will be extended for another three weeks after hosting ambassadors of both countries in Washington.

"The meeting went very well! The United States is going to work with Lebanon in order to help it protect itself from Hezbollah. The ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon will be extended by THREE WEEKS," he said via Truth Social.

In addition, Trump is sending envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner to Islamabad this weekend for talks with Iran's foreign minister, according to reports.

On Thursday, Trump also ordered the U.S. Navy to “shoot and kill” any vessel found placing mines in the Strait of Hormuz, adding that there will be "no hesitation."

Moreover, Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps navy laid more mines in the Strait of Hormuz this week, according to a report by Axios.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) were slightly lower at $93, while Brent (CO1:COM) was last around $104 per barrel.

On the economy, the Consumer Sentiment Index was revised up in April—to 49.8 from the initial 47.6 and 53.3 in March—according to the University of Michigan Survey of Consumers.

data.title: Primis outlines $1.8B mortgage closings while targeting a top 50 mortgage ranking in 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Primis Financial Corp. (FRST) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Dennis Zember framed Q1 profitability around operating results, saying, "in the first quarter, we earned $7.3 million or $0.30 per share" and adding, "on an operating basis, we earned $0.33 per share in the first quarter," while noting Q1 2025 included "a substantial gain on the deconsolidation of Panacea."
  • Zember highlighted balance sheet mix and funding, stating, "Our net interest margin... climbed to 3.43% in the first quarter" and adding, "Deposit growth... came in at just better than 8% with very little of that from the digital platform," alongside, "noninterest-bearing checking accounts growing to $541 million," which he said was "almost 19% higher" year-over-year.
  • Zember emphasized mortgage-led strategy shifts, saying, "Mortgage Warehouse has fully replaced Life Premium Finance at this point" and reporting, "We finished the quarter with about $460 million outstanding," adding that the business delivered "efficiency ratios in the 20s" and that "we could probably double this business in the next 12 to 18 months."
  • On retail mortgage, Zember said, "Retail mortgage had an absolute blowout quarter" and reported, "pretax income in the mortgage group grew to $2.1 million" and "our earnings crept up to 57 basis points on closed volume," adding, "Right now, we believe Primis Mortgage is on track to be a top 50 mortgage company nationwide in 2026."
  • Zember positioned AI as a catalyst, saying, "AI for us is the same kind of opportunity and catalyst that you would expect me to report if we were doing an M&A transaction," and added, "we believe that in a year, we are going to be the undisputed leader amongst banks under $10 billion, using AI to drive operating results... and importantly, fraud prevention."
  • Matthew Switzer tied margin improvement to funding and balance sheet actions, stating, "Net interest income was approximately $32 million" and "Our net interest margin in the first quarter was 3.43%, up from 3.28% last quarter," adding, "we have expectations for margin expansion as we progress through 2026."

Outlook

  • Switzer reiterated margin expansion expectations, saying, "We completed the redemption of $27 million of subordinated debt" and "We also have approximately $400 million of loans repricing in the second half of 2026 and early '27 with a weighted average yield of 4.81% that will add to loan yields."
  • In Q&A, Switzer provided a directional margin range, stating, "we'll see some margin expansion next quarter" and adding, "I would not expect margin to hit 3.6%, but would we hit high 3.4s to 3.5% as we go through the year? Most likely."
  • Zember described profitability ambitions beyond the company’s near-term target, saying, "1% is a good line for us" but also, "aspirationally, we ought to be... 1.25% or better and probably are looking more ROTCE to be something that would get near 15%."

Financial Results

  • Zember reported Q1 earnings, stating, "we earned $7.3 million or $0.30 per share," and linked operating performance to margin and costs: "Driving that were a couple of items, margin mostly and as well as operating expense control."
  • Switzer detailed key P&L line items, stating, "Net interest income was approximately $32 million" and "Noninterest income was $13.6 million," while adding, "Mortgage revenue was solid in Q1 at $10.8 million."
  • On credit, Switzer said, "Our provision this quarter was $1.5 million" and added, "Core net charge-offs remained low at 6 basis points in the first quarter of 2026."
  • On expenses and operating leverage, Switzer stated, "our core expenses were $22 million in the first quarter versus $20.8 million a year ago" and added, "Absent the increased occupancy expense from our recent sale-leaseback transaction, core expenses on this basis would have actually been down year-over-year."

Q&A

  • Woody Lay, KBW: asked what production management expects after a "blowout quarter" and whether mortgage expenses reflected hiring or legal costs; Title Switzer responded, "Nothing unusual on the expense side," and Title Zember said, "we think we're going to do $1.8 billion or so this year" and described profitability drivers including scale and "construction perm."
  • Lay, KBW: asked whether Primis can grow while expanding margin; Title Switzer said, "I think we'll see a little bit more margin expansion" and added, "Most likely" in reference to "high 3.4s to 3.5%" through the year.
  • Lay, KBW: asked about resolution timing on larger NPAs; Title Zember cited improved fundamentals, saying, "both had pretty good quarters on new leases" and "cap rates are improving," adding, "right now... things are trending more positive there."
  • Russell Gunther, Stephens: asked about new loan yields and deposit cost trajectory; Title Switzer said, "on the deposit side, it's probably flat," and added, "in the core bank, probably [ mid-6s ]," while Title Zember discussed rate/term structures across businesses and said, "we could probably take digital down 25 or 30 basis points," but warned, "we don't want to go harvest 30 basis points of deposit cost and then just rely on home loan bank advances."
  • Gunther, Stephens: asked about expense direction including mortgage; Title Switzer said mortgage expense is "so tied to volume" and added, "we... focused on that core expense number, which is around $22 million," and, "I think we'll stay in that kind of $22 million to $23 million range for the year."
  • Gunther, Stephens: asked about ROA glide path and longer-term aspirations; Title Zember said, "we want to build book" and pointed to business scaling and AI, adding, "aspirationally, we ought to be... 1.25% or better" and "near 15%" ROTCE.
  • Christopher Marinac, Brean: asked whether digital deposits are more expensive than brokered and whether growth slows; Title Zember said, "I'm not going to sit here and say that these deposits aren't more expensive," but emphasized a relationship approach and reported, "Our average digital customer has been here probably more than 30 months, closer to 36."
  • Marinac, Brean: asked whether mortgage becomes too large a share of earnings; Title Zember said, "we don't want to be a mortgage company here" and added, "It really probably should be more than 20% of our bottom line," while also saying the core bank must grow so mortgage-related businesses "stay as complement to the bank and not the whole story."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was neutral to slightly positive, with questions focused on sustainability (mortgage run-rate, margin funding, deposit costs, NPA resolution, and mortgage concentration), and phrased as scenario-testing rather than confrontation.
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more expansive in Q&A, with confidence markers including Zember’s "we believe" statements on mortgage ranking and AI leadership, and Switzer’s "we have expectations for margin expansion" plus specific margin range commentary.
  • Versus the prior quarter, management referenced fewer one-time items and spoke more about execution (mortgage production, warehouse scaling, AI efficiencies) rather than Q4’s outsized transaction-related noise.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q4 2025 emphasized one-time items and normalization after "the substantial gain from the sale leaseback" and described a "run rate" of "about $8 million" and "about an 80 basis point ROA"; Q1 2026 emphasized operating metrics, with Zember highlighting "operating basis" EPS and Switzer reporting higher NIM versus Q4.
  • Q4 positioned margin uplift from the restructuring and debt redemption; Q1 carried that forward with Switzer confirming the debt redemption and providing a Q&A range of "high 3.4s to 3.5%" as the year progresses.
  • Analyst focus shifted from Q4’s normalization items (expense noise and special mention migration) to Q1’s forward run-rate questions around mortgage capacity, funding costs, and timing of NPA resolution.

Risks and Concerns

  • Credit and NPAs remained a focal point, with Zember referencing two office CRE relationships and noting lease momentum but also acknowledging the sales cycle is "longer than we want it to be" and that conditions "could change any time."
  • Market volatility in mortgage was cited as a profitability headwind, with Switzer noting Q1 mortgage revenue "would have been even better... if not for the impact of market volatility late in the quarter," and Zember attributing basis-point pressure to "fair value adjustment" impacts.
  • Funding strategy trade-offs were flagged in Q&A, with Zember warning against cutting deposit rates too aggressively and then relying on wholesale funding, saying, "we don't want to be that bank."

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 around improving operating profitability, pointing to a 3.43% net interest margin, deposit mix gains led by noninterest-bearing checking, and mortgage-related expansion—particularly mortgage warehouse scale and retail mortgage profitability—while keeping core expense growth tightly controlled. For 2026, management discussed continued margin expansion drivers (including the subordinated debt redemption and loan repricing), reiterated ambitions around reaching and moving beyond 1% ROA over time, and positioned AI deployment and a more digital core bank as key levers to extend operating leverage and efficiency.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Principal signals confidence in 2026 targets, including 9%-12% EPS growth and 15%-17% ROE, after Q1 margin expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Principal Financial Group (PFG) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “We delivered 13% adjusted non-GAAP earnings per share growth in the first quarter, above the high end of our target range,” driven “primarily” by “favorable underwriting results and improved mortality within our Benefits and Protection business as well as positive market conditions for our fee-based businesses,” Deanna Strable (President, CEO & Chair of the Board) said.

  • “Strong performance and capital generation enabled us to return approximately $375 million of capital to shareholders in the quarter, including $200 million of share repurchases,” and “we also raised our common stock dividend for the 12th consecutive quarter,” Strable said.

  • On retirement momentum, Strable said “total retirement transfer deposits of $12 billion in the quarter grew 35% year-over-year,” while “recurring deposits grew 7%,” and added “$1.7 billion of roll-ins” and “DCIO sales of $2 billion in the quarter.”

  • On asset management, Strable said Investment Management delivered “record gross sales” of “$37 billion,” private markets generated “net inflows of $400 million,” active ETFs had “net inflows of $400 million,” and the firm generated “strong net cash flow of $1.5 billion in the quarter from clients outside the U.S.”

  • “We reported non-GAAP operating earnings of $456 million” and “non-GAAP operating ROE was 16.1%,” while “margin expanded by 190 basis points to 30%,” Joel Pitz (Executive VP & CFO) said.

Outlook

  • “The first quarter was a strong start to the year, and we are well positioned to deliver on our 2026 financial targets,” Pitz said.

  • Variable investment income expectations were reiterated: “We still expect full year 2026 variable investment income to improve relative to 2025 with or without this change,” Pitz said, referring to a reporting reclassification where “we reclassified this noncash expense through realized gains and losses.”

  • On Specialty Benefits profitability, Amy Friedrich (President of Benefits & Protection) said, “When I look at full year outlook, I look at it very favorably with loss ratios expected to emerge at the low end or even slightly below the low end of the range we communicated at outlook.”

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s framing of full-year targets, Q4 2025 included explicit enterprise ranges: “9% to 12% growth in earnings per share, 75% to 85% free capital flow conversion and 15% to 17% return on equity,” Pitz said then; Q1 2026 messaging emphasized being positioned to deliver, without repeating numeric ranges.

Financial Results

  • Pitz said “non-GAAP operating earnings” were “$456 million” (and “$2.07 per share”), while “excluding significant variances” operating earnings were “$479 million” (and “$2.17 per share”); he added “significant variances… had an after-tax impact of $23 million.”

  • Capital and liquidity were described as “over $1.4 billion of excess and available capital,” including “$800 million at the holding company,” “$300 million in our subsidiaries,” and “$350 million in excess of our targeted 375% risk-based capital ratio, which was approximately 400% at quarter end,” Pitz said.

  • Dividend and capital return updates included: “We returned $374 million to shareholders in the first quarter,” and “we announced an $0.82 common stock dividend payable in the second quarter… a $0.02 increase from the dividend paid in the first quarter,” Pitz said.

  • Segment details included RIS pretax operating earnings of “$318 million” and Investment Management record gross sales “up 21%” year-over-year; International Pension AUM reached “a record $160 billion” with “positive $500 million” net cash flow; Benefits and Protection pretax operating earnings were “$177 million,” Pitz said.

Q&A

  • Ryan Krueger, KBW: Asked about favorable Specialty Benefits underwriting and the outlook; Amy Friedrich (President Friedrich): “The underwriting performance was really strong this quarter… primarily Group Life and Dental,” adding, “second quarter does tend to be the seasonally highest for Dental… the overall SBD loss ratio does rise a bit in second quarter.”

  • Ryan Krueger, KBW: Asked why Investment Management redemptions rose despite record gross sales; Kamal Bhatia (President & CEO, Principal Asset Management): “Redemption activity… concentrated among a very small number of U.S. equity… mutual funds… driven by changes in asset allocation and advisory business models,” and “as redemption activity normalizes, I would expect our nonaffiliated NCF profile to improve for the balance of the year.”

  • Wesley Carmichael, Wells Fargo: Asked whether Individual Life earnings power changed; Amy Friedrich (President Friedrich): “We definitely feel like we saw some positive volatility in mortality this quarter,” but “towards the lower end of that range for a full year expectation… is what I would be thinking about.”

  • Wilma Burdis, Raymond James: Asked about lower PRT sales; Christopher Littlefield (President of Retirement & Income Solutions) said Q4 2025 was “very strong… over $1 billion of PRT sales,” which “reducing the pipelines in the first quarter,” and “we kind of expect this year to be fairly similar to 2025 when it comes to PRT.”

  • Michael Ward, UBS: Asked about a small dental acquisition and impact; Amy Friedrich (President Friedrich): “The answer is no” on Q1 impact, and “Any benefit… will start showing up in second quarter and beyond.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to skeptical on flow durability and mix (redemptions, mortality sustainability, PRT softness, macro lag), with questions such as “why do you… been seeing higher redemptions?” (Krueger) and whether Life strength was “onetime-ish” (Carmichael).

  • Management tone was slightly positive and confident in prepared remarks and measured in Q&A; Pitz said “we are well positioned to deliver on our 2026 financial targets,” while responses to volatility leaned on normalization and seasonality (e.g., Bhatia: “as redemption activity normalizes”; Friedrich: “seasonally highest for Dental”).

  • Versus the prior quarter, Q4 2025 carried a more explicitly target-and-outlook-heavy tone (“9% to 12%,” “75% to 85%,” “15% to 17%”), while Q1 2026 emphasized execution proof-points, capital return, and segment drivers.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 2026 emphasized operational drivers behind results (underwriting and mortality improvement, retirement transfer deposits, record gross sales) and detailed segment profitability; Q4 2025 emphasized full-year performance, portfolio optimization actions, and formal 2026 target ranges.

  • Management messaging shifted from Q4’s outlook construction (including seasonality guidance for expenses and segment targets) to Q1’s validation of trajectory, with Pitz stating Q1 was “a strong start to the year.”

  • Analyst focus shifted from Q4 topics like portfolio optimization and free capital flow mechanics to Q1 topics around Investment Management redemptions, Specialty Benefits sustainability, and flow outlooks in RIS.

Risks and Concerns

  • Pitz highlighted industry attention on private credit: “There has been heightened attention recently on the insurance industry's exposure to private credit,” adding “the vast majority of our private fixed income securities are investment grade with minimal exposure to direct lending,” and that the portfolio is “appropriately aligned with the liquidity profile of our liabilities.”

  • Management acknowledged flow lumpiness and seasonality in retirement and benefits; Littlefield said sales and transfer deposits follow “the historical pattern where Q1 is our strongest,” and Friedrich flagged dental seasonality where the loss ratio “does rise a bit in second quarter.”

Final Takeaway

Principal’s leadership presented Q1 as an execution-driven start to 2026, pointing to 13% adjusted non-GAAP EPS growth, margin expansion to 30%, and $374 million returned to shareholders alongside an increased $0.82 dividend. In Q&A, management framed key investor-sensitive swing factors—Specialty Benefits seasonality, Investment Management redemptions concentrated in a small U.S. equity mutual fund set, and retirement flow lumpiness—as manageable, while reiterating confidence in delivering the company’s 2026 financial targets.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Palantir Helps IRS mine large volumes of data – The Intercept
data.description:
Palantir (PLTR) is reportedly helping the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) analyze dozens of data sets on American citizens to investigate a vast range of financial crimes.

The IRS’ Criminal Investigation Division has used the military contractor’s Lead and Case Analytics platform since 2018 to analyze and aggregate diverse lists of federal databases and data sets, The Intercept reported.

American Oversight, a non-profit watchdog group, obtained public records detailing Palantir’s IRS contract and shared them with the Intercept. The public records revealed the vast amount of data plugged into Palantir’s software.

According to the article, the documents revealed that Palantir’s Lead and Case Analytics platform allows the IRS to search and visualize connections from millions of records with thousands of links between databases maintained by other federal agencies and the IRS. The data includes Affordable Care Act data, individual tax forms and tax returns, bank statements and transactions, and other available data compiled by the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network of the Treasury Department.

The documents suggest that its reach extends to cryptocurrencies, including Bitcoin, Litecoin, Ethereum, and Ripple.

According to the contract documents, the IRS has paid over $130M to the military contractor for its services to date.

Palantir and the IRS did not immediately respond to a Seeking Alpha request for comment.

American Oversight director Chioma Chukwu said in a statement to The Intercept that the real concern is the consolidation of vast amounts of sensitive personal data into a single system with minimal transparency, especially one built and operated by a contractor like Palantir, whose business model is premised on integrating data and expanding surveillance capabilities.

“When the government can map relationships, track behavior, and generate investigative leads across data sets at this scale, the question isn’t just what it can do—it's who it will be used against,” Chukwu said to The Intercept. “Entrusting that infrastructure to a company known for opaque, security-state deployments only heightens those risks.”
